title: Introduction to Computer Organization and Assembly Language

blocks.title: Academic Integrity
blocks.content: Plagiarized assignments and quizzes will be awarded straight zero.
Textbooks: (1) Computer Organization and Architecture – William Stallings, 10th Ed. (2) Assembly Language for Intel-Based Computers – Kip Irvine, 7th Ed.

blocks.definition: The end-to-end structure of a computer system that determines how its components interact to execute the machine's purpose (processing data), without reference to actual technical implementation.

blocks.content: Architecture describes attributes visible to the programmer — instruction set, number of bits for data representation, I/O mechanisms, and addressing techniques.

items: Instruction set (e.g., is there a multiply instruction?)
Number of bits used for data representation
I/O mechanisms
Addressing techniques

blocks.content: Architecture = what the programmer sees. Organization = how it is implemented internally.

left.title: Architecture
content: Instruction set, data representation, I/O, addressing
Example: Is there a multiply instruction?

right.title: Organization
content: Example: Is multiply done by a dedicated unit or by repeated addition?
Can change across chip generations without changing architecture

blocks.title: Real-World Example: Intel x86
blocks.content: All Intel processors (8086 → Core i9) share the same x86 architecture (ISA) but differ in organization — number of cores, cache size, clock speed, manufacturing process (e.g., 14nm vs 7nm).
A family of computers can share the same architecture while having different organizations — leading to different price and performance characteristics.
ISA (Instruction Set Architecture) is often used interchangeably with Architecture. ISA defines instruction formats, opcodes, registers, memory effects, and instruction execution control.

left.title: Structure
content: The way in which components relate to each other

right.title: Function
content: The operation of individual components as part of the structure

blocks.content: There are four fundamental computer functions:

rows: Performing arithmetic/logical operations on data of various forms
Short-term (RAM, on-the-fly) and long-term (files on disk)
I/O with peripherals or data communication with remote devices
CPU manages execution order, resource access, memory, and I/O

blocks.definition: Data transfer between the computer and a directly connected peripheral device.
Data transfer over longer distances to or from a remote device.

blocks.content: Control – Resource Management includes: accessing memory, controlling I/O devices, and allocating processing power.
The Von Neumann architecture is the foundational model for modern computers. It consists of a CPU, memory, and I/O — all connected by buses.

items: CPU: Contains ALU, Control Unit, and Registers
Main Memory: Stores both instructions and data
I/O Devices: Keyboard, mouse, display, network, storage
System Bus: Connects CPU, memory, and I/O

blocks.content: Key IAS Registers inside the CPU:

rows: Holds data being transferred to/from memory
Holds address of memory location to access
Holds address of the next instruction to fetch

blocks.content: Know all 6 IAS registers, their abbreviations, and their roles — frequently tested.
The Instruction Cycle is the basic operational process of a Von Neumann computer. It has two main phases: Fetch and Execute.

items: FETCH: CPU reads the instruction from memory at the address stored in PC
LOAD: Instruction is placed into the Instruction Register (IR)
INCREMENT: PC is incremented to point to the next instruction (unless a jump occurs)
DECODE: Control unit decodes the instruction — identifies opcode and operands
EXECUTE: The decoded instruction is carried out by ALU or control unit
REPEAT: Cycle restarts from Fetch

blocks.content: Types of Execute Cycle operations:

rows: Data transfer between CPU and main memory
Data transfer between CPU and I/O module
Arithmetic or logical operation on data

blocks.content: Fetch Cycle sequence: PC → MAR → Memory → MBR → IR → PC incremented. This register flow is a common exam question.

left.title: High-Level Languages
content: Compiled by a compiler to machine code

right.title: Low-Level Languages
content: Machine Language: binary (1s and 0s), executed directly by hardware

blocks.definition: A tool that translates assembly language code into machine code (binary).
A tool that translates high-level language programs into machine code — either directly or indirectly via an assembler.

blocks.title: Translation Example
blocks.content: D = A * B + 10 in C compiles to: mov eax, A / mul B / add eax, 10 / mov D, eax in x86 Assembly, which becomes hex machine code like A1 00404000...
Assembly language is NOT portable — it is tied to a specific CPU architecture. High-level languages are portable because compilers handle architecture differences.

items: Accessibility to system hardware — useful for writing device drivers and embedded systems
Space efficiency — write compact, optimized code
Time efficiency — understand and tune program performance
Understanding sources of program inefficiency

rows: Portion of the computer that fetches and executes instructions; contains ALU, control unit, and registers
A physical silicon chip containing one or more cores; interprets and executes instructions
An individual processing unit on a processor chip; functionally equivalent to a single-CPU system
A processor chip containing multiple cores

blocks.content: A Core consists of: Arithmetic & Logic Unit (ALU), Instruction logic, Load/Store logic, L1 data cache, L1 instruction cache, and L2 cache.

blocks.definition: A small, fast memory layer between the processor and main memory. Stores frequently used data to speed up access. Organized in levels: L1 (closest to core, fastest) → L2 → L3 (shared, largest but slowest among cache levels).

blocks.content: Know the difference: CPU (logical concept) vs Processor (physical chip) vs Core (individual processing unit on a chip).

blocks.definition: The ability of a modern processor to execute multiple instructions simultaneously. Early processors (e.g., Intel 8086) needed 3–10 cycles per instruction; modern processors sustain 2–4 instructions per cycle.

Special hardware that allows a single instruction to perform multiple parallel operations. Example: Intel/AMD processors can add 8 pairs of single-precision floats simultaneously with one instruction.

blocks.content: An instruction word is divided into fields that tell the CPU what to do and what data to use.

rows: Specifies data or addresses to use

blocks.title: Instruction Format Layout
blocks.content: [ Opcode | Operand(s) ] — The opcode field defines WHAT to do; the operand field defines WHERE the data is or WHAT the data is.
A program is a sequence of instructions. Each instruction requires a different set of control signals to execute.
The x86 is the most widely used ISA in personal computers and servers.

items: Began with 8-bit Intel 8086 in 1978
Evolved through 16-bit, 32-bit, to today's 64-bit processors (Core i3, i5, i7, i9)
All models share the same x86 ISA — ensures code compatibility across generations
Clones developed by AMD and Cyrix support the same architecture with different organization
Architecture spans from tablets to supercomputers

blocks.title: Key Takeaway
blocks.content: Same architecture (x86 ISA) but different organizations across generations — this is why old software still runs on modern Intel chips.
